# Welcome to “Sword World”!

**Welcome to “Sword World”!** 

4

# What is a Role-Playing Game (RPG)?

## **What is a Role-Playing Game (RPG)?** 

_Role-Playing_ means _to act out a role_ . A role-playing game (RPG), as the name suggests, progresses by having each participant in the game play out an assigned _role_ . 

Participants in an RPG can be roughly divided into two groups: One is the **game master** ( **GM** ), while the other are the **players** . RPGs are played by several people, but there is only one game master while the rest are the players. 

Before playing a game, the game master must prepare what’s called a **scenario** . You may either create your own, or you may use an existing one. A number of scenario collections have already been produced for _Sword World RPG_ , and will continue to be released in the future. 

The game proceeds according to a prepared scenario. The game master is the one who facilitates the scenario, while the players _play the role_ -- of the **characters** --in the scenario. If you think of the game master as a director and the players as actors, it's similar to making a movie or a play. 

However, there is something that’s very different from film or theater. Which is: the players do _not_ know the scenario. They _only_ know the characters they’re taking on.  The players must make their characters act based on their abilities and characteristics. The game master's scenario does _not_ provide any dialogue, etc., for the characters. 

Let's give a real example. **GM** is the game master, and **A** , **B** , and **C** are different players. 

**GM** ： You guys were traveling and you arrived at a small village. You're now resting at a tavern now that the sun is setting. Then, about five or six unsavorylooking people enter. They start to have fun by making nasty remarks at the 

poster girl at the bar, much to her annoyance. 

**A** ： That doesn't sound good... okay, I'll give them a little warning. 

**B** ： Come on, if you get yourself hurt, you'll look like a fool. Just ignore them. 

**A** ： What do you mean? That would soil the name of a hero of justice! 

**B** ： You're the only one who thinks he’s a hero of justice, aren't you?” 

**C** ： .......say, is the poster girl pretty? 

**GM** ： She is. 

**C** ： What do the thugs look like? 

**GM** ： They have very dirty leather armor and shortswords. 

**B** ： They sound like bandits. That Thieves Guild scares me. 

**C** ： Even I have the backing of the Thieves Guild (laughs). These guys don't seem like a big bunch, so I say we be "heroes of justice". Hey you guys, leave her alone! 

**A** ： That's my line! 

**B** ： I’ll just pretend I’m not with you guys… 

**GM** ： But you're all sitting at the same table, so I don’t think they’ll believe you. (laughs) One of the thugs, who looks particularly rough, snarls “What, you have the gall to complain about us - the Brando family?!”. 

**B** ： Uh-oh... we're in trouble… 

**A** ： Bring it on! 

Thus, the game proceeds. In this case, the scenario prepared by the game master does _not_ say _there will be a fight with thugs (the Brando family)_ . It only says _thugs will misbehave in the tavern_ , then it's up to the players to decide what they'll do about it. If the players were all like player **B** , there would've been no conflict. The conflict only arose because 

the players wanted it. So, what will be the outcome of this fight? 

Here is where another major feature of role-playing games comes into play. That is… 

Not knowing what the results will be. 

That is to say: 

Neither the game master nor the players know whether the characters will be able to kick the thugs out of the tavern and become _heroes of justice_ , or get beaten by the thugs and instead become _heroes of comedy_ . 

Even in real life, if you start a fight, you won't know who will win without actually fighting. The only thing you should know is _Who is considered stronger?_ . 

Role-playing games reproduce this uncertainty through random numbers (mainly dice) and numerical values representing character attributes. 

In other words, based on a number that represents a character's strength, a result is determined by rolling the dice. 

Sometimes it’s like this: 

**Player** ： (rolls) Hey, I got a good roll, my attack is a hit! 

Other times, it’s like this: 

**Player** ： (rolls) Agh, my roll was bad. My attack failed! 

In this way, due to two types of randomness, _player's choice_ and _random numbers (dice)_ , the actual game can display a wide degree of fluctuation, sometimes the "story" will evolve beyond the expectations of the game master. 

In a role-playing game, you can create a story, as well as enjoy the story as a spectator. 

If you enjoy the story in this way, you can say that you've experienced the best part of a role-playing game. 

5

# The Meaning of the Rulebook ## **The Meaning of the Rulebook** This book is a rulebook for the roleplaying game entitled _Sword World RPG_ . As stated, a role-playing game progresses according to a scenario prepared by the game master, as well as the players' decisions and random numbers. This rulebook is here to help you with that. A role-playing game, like most fiction, takes place in a fictional world. As mentioned earlier, Forcelia, the world you’ll deal with in _Sword World RPG_ , is different from our real world. There is magic, and creatures that can only be found in fiction will come to life. However, even fictional worlds have their own laws. _Because it's magic_ does not mean something is all-powerful, and _because they're monsters_ does not mean all monsters are equally strong or scary. _Because you're a hero_ doesn't mean you can do everything. Each has their own characteristics and limitations. For example, some people may have spectacular muscle strength, while others may be less fortunate. A weapon wielded by someone with superior strength can do more damage than a weapon wielded by someone who is not blessed with such strength. However, with training and experience, even those with inferior talents should be able to fight admirably. To the average person, a tiger would be a terrifying creature. However, the The rules set forth in this book are the fundaments that characterize Forcelia. If you don't understand them, you won't be able to grasp the characteristics of Forcelia, but not _all_ of Forcelia's laws are written in this book. Please understand this. In making decisions, be sure to add your own _common sense_ to the rules. heroes present in _Sword World RPG_ are capable of fighting as well as or even better than a tiger. The rules exist to reproduce such things. To what extent does a person's muscular aptitude affect their ability to fight? How much does experience make up for it? How do tigers, ordinary people, and heroes differ in strength? It should be noted that, as a general principle of role-playing games, the game master makes the final decisions. In all cases, the word of the game master is absolute in the progress of the game. You may think of the rules as the _law_ and the game master as the _judge_ who applies it. It is the _rules_ that represent that. In this sense, the rulebook is written to reproduce all the laws and details in the fictional world. If decisions are made according to the rulebook, success or failure of the characters' abilities and actions in the fictional world can all be determined with certainty. **==> picture [219 x 243] intentionally omitted <==** **----- Start of picture text -----**
is
.
**----- End of picture text -----**
Unfortunately, however, this is merely an _ideal_ . For example, it was stated that Forcelia follows different laws than the real world. Indeed, they are different in many ways. Therefore, in order to be a complete rulebook, _all_ of its laws would have to be written. That is to say, _an apple that falls from a tree lands on the ground_ . This is neither possible nor realistic. Rather, it's better to think of the rules set forth by the book as merely a guide. For things that the rules cannot fully define, please use your own **common sense** to make decisions. Even though it's not written in the book, _an apple that falls from a tree lands on the ground._ 6

# What You’ll Need to Play Sword World RPG

## **The Structure of the Rulebook** 

This book consists of five major parts. 

**==> picture [22 x 34] intentionally omitted <==**

## **Welcome to Sword World!** 

This is the part you are reading right now. It's a brief introduction to _Sword World RPG_ . If you're not familiar with role-playing games or _Sword World RPG_ , please start reading here. 

**==> picture [21 x 35] intentionally omitted <==**

## **Part 1 Player Section** 

This is the part for the players, one of the two groups of people needed to enjoy an RPG. It introduces the minimum rules you should remember when playing _Sword World RPG_ . 

**==> picture [21 x 34] intentionally omitted <==**

## **Part 2 Game Master Section** 

This is the part for the game master. A game master requires a deeper mastery of an RPG compared to players. When you’re a game master, you will have to remember rules that you wouldn’t have to remember when you’re a player. Rules that aren’t necessary from a player's point of view are included here. 

Therefore, if you're solely a player, you _don't_ have to force yourself to memorize the rules of this part. Conversely, a game master _must_ understand the rules included here in addition to those included in Part 1. 

**==> picture [21 x 34] intentionally omitted <==**

## **Part 3 Optional Rules** 

These are rules designed to make playing _Sword World RPG_ more interesting. However, having additional rules to memorize often gets in the way 

of the speedy development that is the life of an RPG. 

The rules in this section are for those who already have an understanding of _Sword World RPG_ and are able to play with ease. It's _not_ recommended for inexperienced players to use the rules here from the beginning. 

**==> picture [21 x 34] intentionally omitted <==**

## **Guide to Forcelia and Alecrast** 

_Sword World RPG_ is set on the continent of **Alecrast** , a part of the world called **Forcelia** . This part is a brief overview of Forcelia as a whole, the history of Alecrast, and various regions. 

In order to play an RPG, it's best to have all participants understand the background setting, and proceed with the game implicitly based on this **common understanding** . This part should help you with that. 

## **What You’ll Need to Play Sword World RPG** 

When playing _Sword World RPG_ , you will have to write and rewrite many things during the game. So you’ll need pencils, erasers, notepads, etc. 

You will also need Character Sheets. A Character Sheet is used to keep track of a character who acts in a fictional world (Alecrast in the case of _Sword World RPG_ ) as the player's alter ego. These are needed per the number of people. Please copy and use the sheets included in this book. 

other RPGs, incorporates chance and uses dice to resolve various actions, in order to make the game more fun. The dice used are ordinary six-sided dice. Since two dice are often used at a time, it is advisable to bring two dice per person. 

From here on in this book, rolling dice will be referred to as _rolling 1D_ or _rolling 2D_ . When you _roll 1D_ , roll one die and use the result that comes up. When you _roll 2D_ , roll two dice at the same time and use the total of both numbers (2-12). 

## **Handling fractions** 

Due to the nature of the game, there are situations in _Sword World RPG_ where math calculations must be made. If division results in a fraction, the fraction is either rounded up or rounded down. Which one is clearly stated for each situation. However, if it's _not_ mentioned, the fraction should _always_ be rounded up. 

Along with pens and pencils, you must not forget the dice. As mentioned before, _Sword World RPG_ , like many 

7

heading_path: Books Related to Sword World RPG
text:
# Books Related to Sword World RPG

## **Books Related to Sword World RPG** 

Here, we'll introduce some books that will help you understand _Sword World RPG_ . You do not need all of these, but knowing their contents can make playing _Sword World RPG_ more enjoyable. 

**==> picture [21 x 35] intentionally omitted <==**

## **1. Replay Collections** 

The _Sword World RPG Replay Collections_ , published by Fujimi Dragon Books, are a good way to learn how to actually play the game. The progress of the game is reproduced via conversation between the game master and the players. 

Once you read these, you will quickly understand how the game is meant to progress. 

There are three points to be aware of, however. 

First, _Sword World RPG Replay Collections_ ① _~_ ⑨ are _not_ based on this rulebook, but rather on the previously released _old_ version of the rules (see below). Since some of the rules in this book you’re reading right now have been revised, there are rare cases where the descriptions are contradictory. When you play, please follow what's written in _these_ rules. 

Second, although the _Duda RPG Replay Collection_ series uses the rules of _Sword World RPG_ , it is a variant that emphasizes the aspects of humor, fantasy and mystery. As a game, it’s nearly the same, but in this series, modified rules not found in _Sword World RPG_ , such as **inept detective checks** , have been introduced to demonstrate its uniqueness. So please note that even though it's 99% Sword World RPG, it's still not 100% (for more information on this, please read the Introduction and Commentary sections of _Duda RPG Replay Collection_ ① _: The Lost Detective Appears!_ ). 

Finally, these replays are based on actual play and reproduction of the 

situations, but have been cut or edited for redundancy and other aspects in order to prioritize readability. Some rule errors that occurred during the game have been corrected and dealt with, especially in areas where they do not have a major impact. In actual play, things do not go as smoothly as this. A replay should _not_ be considered a complete manual, but merely a reference. 

**==> picture [21 x 34] intentionally omitted <==**

## **2. Scenario Collections** 

The _Sword World RPG Scenario Collections_ , published by Fujimi Dragon Books, save game masters the trouble of creating their own scenarios. Not only will they save you from creating scenarios, but they will also serve as a useful model when you do create your own. 

Please note that some of these follow the _old_ version of rules. However, since there are almost no revisions, they can be used nearly as-is. 

Although the details of monsters and magical items listed in individual scenarios may differ from the rules in this book, when using them, please play with the details as described in the scenario, with the exception of item prices. This is because the information in this book has been modified for more general use. For this reason, there may be times when game balance within the scenario is not fully supported. Please follow this rule for item prices only. 

**==> picture [21 x 35] intentionally omitted <==**

## **3. World Guide** 

The _Sword World RPG World Guide_ , published by Fujimi Shobo, is a comprehensive introduction to the continent of Alecrast, the background setting of the game. It is very useful when creating your own scenarios, and for those who are mainly players, it is also 

effective to have a general outline of the world. 

## **4. Sword World Novels and Short Story Collections** 

**==> picture [21 x 35] intentionally omitted <==**

_Sword World Novels_ and _Sword World Short Story Collections_ published by Fujimi Fantasia Bunko are a suitable introduction to the world of _Sword World RPG_ . 

It’ll be helpful to know what the setting of _Sword World RPG_ is like and what kind of thoughts the people who live there are thinking. 

However, among these, the _Duda Series_ and _Dawn of Chaos_ `Ⅰ` _-_ `Ⅳ` are slightly different. The former, as mentioned in the Replay Collection section, is a humorous mystery variant, so the books emphasize this aspect. The latter is set not on the continent of Alecrast, the main setting of _Sword World RPG_ , but rather in a land called **Chaos Land** . All of these require some ingenuity to handle with this rulebook alone. However, the underlying setting of Forcelia is still common. 

**==> picture [21 x 34] intentionally omitted <==**

## **5. Sword World RPG Adventures** 

The _Sword World RPG Adventures_ series, published by Fujimi Dragon Books, are collections of ideas from readers who play _Sword World RPG_ , in the form of novels. Similar to the _Sword World Novels/Short Story Collections_ , these are a good way to learn about the setting. 

**==> picture [21 x 35] intentionally omitted <==**

## **6. Collection Series** 

The _Collection Series_ , such as _Monster Collection_ published by Fujimi Dragon Books, are a good way to learn the terminology used within the game in _Sword World RPG_ . 

8

This game uses terms such as _goblin_ and _broadsword_ , which appear frequently in fantasy games, but are otherwise not so common. Explaining each of these terms one by one would not only make the rulebook thicker, but would also hinder understanding of the rules themselves. 

Therefore, this rulebook keeps explanations of such terms to a minimum. To learn more about these terms, please refer to the _Collection Series_ . 

**==> picture [21 x 35] intentionally omitted <==**

## **7. Old Version of the Rules** 

Three volumes have been published by Fujimi Dragon Books: _Sword World RPG_ , and _Sword World RPG Advanced Rules Part 1_ and _2_ . These are the rulebooks for _Sword World RPG_ , that is, they serve exactly the same purpose as this book. This book is a re-edited and partially revised version of the contents of these three books, so they're unnecessary if you’re holding this in your hands. 

**==> picture [21 x 35] intentionally omitted <==**

## **8. Game Master Screen** 

The _Sword World RPG Game Master Screen_ is a collection of charts (or tables) that you will need in order to play this game. This can be useful for smooth game progress. 

However, it is in accordance with the _old_ version of the rules mentioned above. In this book, some rules have been revised and some tables have been slightly changed. Therefore, it's advisable to make some modifications when using this. For example, you can copy the revised tables and paste them over the old ones. 

In addition to these, _Sword World RPG_ is scheduled to feature a variety of other related works. It will also continue to be supported in magazines published by Fujimi Shobo, such as _Dragon Magazine_ . 

**==> picture [317 x 602] intentionally omitted <==**

9

# Part 1: Players’ Section

**Part 1: Players’ Section** 

10

# Chapter 1: Character

**Chapter 1: Character** 

11

# 1.1 Ability Scores ## **Ability Scores** ## **1.1** Just as there are many different kinds of people in the real world, strong (or weak), smart (or dumb), etc., there are many different kinds of people in Forcelia. In Sword World RPG, these basic characteristics of an adventurer are represented by numbers called **ability scores** . There are six types of ability scores: **dexterity** , **agility** , **intelligence** , **strength** , **life force** , and **mental power** . The higher the number for each, the better the ability. Ability scores apply not only to humans, but also to the various non-human races that become adventurers. Human ability scores range from 4-24 (14 on average) for each. large weapons, heavy armor, etc., high strength is required. ## 【 **Life Force** 】 Life force represents how well an adventurer can withstand physical injury. The higher this number, the more robust the adventurer is. ## 【 **Mental Power** 】 Mental power represents the adventurer's willpower and perseverance. Using magic is hard work, and one cannot hope to cast much magic without a high mental capacity. Mental power is also related to magic resistance. ## 【 **Dexterity** 】 ## **Loss of Ability Scores and Its Impact** Dexterity represents the dexterity of an adventurer's hands. It relates to a variety of tasks that involve the use of one's hands. This is especially important when wielding weapons in combat. While adventuring, adventurers often suffer physical and mental injuries and fatigue. In _Sword World RPG_ , this is represented by a loss of life force and mental power (point reduction). If either life force or mental power fall to 0 or less, the adventurer will fall unconscious and be unable to act. The four other ability scores of dexterity, agility, intelligence, and strength are rarely reduced, but if this does happen and these ability scores _do_ fall to 0 or less, the adventurer will still become disabled. At this time, whether they're conscious or not depends on the situation. ## 【 **Agility** 】 Agility represents an adventurer's physical balance and agility. It's related to working with the whole body. In combat, it is important when evading enemy attacks. ## 【 **Intelligence** 】 Intelligence represents the speed of an adventurer's mind and the amount of knowledge they hold, or in other words, their so-called smarts. It also represents the ability to perceive things, such as finding hidden objects and sensing abnormalities spur of the moment. Above all, it is important when using magic, a major attraction in fantasy. If life force and mental power are reduced by wounds or exhaustion, they will recover once the wounds are treated or the exhaustion is wiped away. There are various conditions for these reductions and recoveries, which you will discover as you read through this book. ## 【 **Strength** 】 ## 【 **Ability Bonuses** 】 Strength represents an adventurer's muscular strength. If you want to use An ability score value is related to the success or failure of an action that an adventurer tries to perform, but in _Sword World RPG_ , success or failure is determined by a **bonus** derived from the ability score, rather than the ability score itself. The result of each ability score divided by 6 (rounded down) will be the bonus for that ability score (Table 1-1: Ability Bonuses). Each of these numbers is referred to as a _dexterity bonus_ , _intelligence bonus_ , etc., using the name of the ability score. For example, it would say something like _an adventurer with 14 dexterity has a dexterity bonus of +2_ (of course, the bonus is always 0 or a positive number, but by nature it's usually written ±0, +1, +2...... etc.) When a character's ability score increases or decreases, even temporarily, the ability bonus increases or decreases accordingly. However, temporary reductions do _not_ affect modifiers for only two scores: life force and mental power. **Table 1-1: Ability Bonuses** |AbilityScore|Bonus| |---|---| |2~5|±0| |6~11|+1| |12~17|+2| |18~23|+3| |24~29|+4| |Add +1 for every +6 in ability scores
thereafer|| 12

# 1.2 Races

## **Races** 

## **1.2** 

Humans are not the only adventurers in Forcelia. Next, we'll explain the races that are relatively common as adventurers in Forcelia, though they’re not as common as humans. 

Players are free to choose from humans or any of these races to be their alter ego, an adventurer. 

## 【 **Dwarf** 】 

Dwarves were once members of the fae tribe who lived in the fairy world, but now they have fully immersed into the world inhabited by humans (which we call the material world) and no longer have the ability to travel to and from the fairy world. Their lives are no different from those of humans. 

They are about 120 cm tall, round and plump, and have bulbous noses. Men often have long beards on their chins, while women do not. Their hair ranges from brown to dark brown and their eyes are a similar color. Their skin color is dark, almost brown. 

They can see things in the dark as well as they can in daylight. They are also very dexterous with their hands, and excel in such techniques as craftsmanship and blacksmithing. Many of their works are stunning and cannot be imitated by humans. Dwarves usually live in human cities and villages using their own skills, but there are several cities and villages where only dwarves live. 

They have a very strong sense of tribalism, respecting the rules of their tribe and seldom violating them. They mainly worship the gods of the earth, with **Braki** as their main deity, who is also the god of smithing. 

The life span of a dwarf is longer than that of a human, thought to be around 200 years. 

Their rule characteristics are as follows: 

2. Among the craftsman general skill, has any one of the following at level 5: carpentry, smithing, woodworking, stonemasonry, gem crafting (details are explained under 7.3: Craftsman Skill, see p. 126). 

3. Unable to learn ancient magic or spirit magic. 

## 【 **Grassrunner** 】 

Grassrunners were originally members of the fae tribe who lived in the meadows. However, they are very curious and many have abandoned the fairy world to become residents of human society. They have already lost the ability to return to the fairy world, but as fae of the meadows, they have a truly diverse range of abilities. 

They are about 1 meter tall and have the appearance of a human child. Other than their slightly pointed ears, they are not much different from humans. Their hair varies from golden to tan. Their eyes have a similar color. Their skin color is predominantly black or reddish. 

They’re not in the habit of settling in one place. They only stay in one place for a while only to raise a few children, and once the children are grown, they travel again. They usually lead a nomadic lifestyle, either alone or in family units. Of course, they have no concept of tribes, nor do they like to live in cities. Their personalities are very cheerful and simple. Therefore, they have no custom of believing in gods at all. What they desire are the various experiences and incidents one encounters while traveling, so for this reason they may join groups of adventurers. 

Grassrunners have a life span of about 200 years. 

Their rule characteristics are as follows: 

1. Has 1 level in the thief skill. 

3. Can communicate with plants and insects. 

4. Cannot learn any runes. 

## 【 **Elf** 】 

Elves are members of the fae tribe who live in the forests, and are the envy of humans because of their beautiful appearance and immortality (no life span). 

Their most distinctive feature is their long, slender, pointy ears. They're about 160 cm tall and slim. Their body hair is thin, and even men do not grow beards. Their hair color ranges from golden to chestnut, sometimes silver. Their skin color is near white and their eyes are pale blue or green. They are also close as a species to humans and both can produce offspring. These children are called **halfelves** . 

They live a very closed-off life in the forest. They do not like outsiders, nor do they want to be ruled by others. As such, they may act aggressively toward intruders into the forest. They rarely leave the forests where they live, but occasionally the odd elf will show up in town. They do not believe in any gods. 

One of the reasons humans envy elves is their _immortality_ , which is a characteristic of an elite (ancient) race called **high elves** . However, these high elves avoid contact with humans even more so than common elves. Elves who appear in the cities of humans are for the most part ordinary (newer species) elves, bound to a life span of about 1000 years rather than immortality. 

The rule characteristics of elves are as follows: 

      1. Has 1 level in the shaman skill 

      2. Unable to learn holy magic. 

   2. Has 1 level in the ranger skill. 

1. Can see in the dark. 

13

# 1.3 Skills

## 【 **Half-Elf** 】 

A half-elf is one who is born of a human and an elf. Humans and elves are close as species and are able to have children, as explained in the elf section. 

Half-elves combine characteristics of both humans and elves. Their ears are slightly pointed, but they also have a spot where a beard grows. They are almost the same height as humans, but are more delicate. 

## **Skills** 

## **1.3** 

In addition to ability scores, _Sword World RPG_ also uses numeric values called **skills** to describe the characteristics of characters. Skills have a greater impact on a character's actions than ability scores. This chapter explains the basics of skills. 

## **1.3.1** 

## **Skill Basics** 

There are several types of skills, which are divided into several groups. For each skill, a character's proficiency is represented by a **level** from 0~10. 0 levels indicates that someone is completely unrelated to that skill, while 10 levels represents the highest proficiency that a normal human can attain. 

Skills are referred to by names such as _fighter_ , _thief_ , _merchant_ , etc. As you can see, they have a strong connotation of professional job functions rather than skills. It is also possible for one character to acquire (have levels in) more than one skill. 

Those who have these skills are often referred to by their skill names. So instead of _someone who has the fighter skill_ , they are simply called _fighters_ . 

Half-elves have a longer life span than humans due to their elven characteristics, with a life span of around 200 years. 

Half-elves vary in their traits depending on whether they were raised by humans or by elves. When raised by elves, they're similar to those of elves. When raised by humans, they're not all that different from humans. 

## **1.3.2** 

## **Skill Types** 

Skills are grouped into several types. Below is an explanation. 

First, skills are divided into two types: **adventurer skills** and **general skills** . Adventurer skills, as the name implies, are acquired by player characters, the adventurers, or by equivalent characters. There are eight of these: **fighter** , **thief** , **priest** , **sorcerer** , **shaman** , **sage** , **ranger** , and **bard** . There are also two adventurer skills that _cannot_ be acquired by a player character: **dark priest** and **dragon priest** . 

General skills are skills of an ordinary person as opposed to an adventurer, such as **merchant** , **farmer** , etc. Some of these are useful during an adventure, and it is well worth it for an adventurer to acquire general skills. 

Each skill consists of a number of abilities appropriate to the skill name, and having a **skill level** (1 skill level or higher) means that you can demonstrate those abilities. 

The table below shows the names and types of each skill (Table 1-2: Skill Types). 

## 《 **Changeling** 》 

Half-elves are not a single generation of mixed blood, but are free to have offspring. The child will be a full elf, a half-elf, or a human. There is no such thing as inheriting only 1/4 of the characteristics of one or the other. 

Even among humans, elves and halfelves can be born from humans who have had elven blood in their past due to intermittent inheritance. These children are called **changelings** and are looked down upon in human society. 

## **1.3.3** 

## **Adventurer Level** 

In Sword World RPG, a character's capability as an adventurer is determined by their adventurer skill level. The highest level among the adventurer skills that a character has is called that character's **adventurer level** . For example, a character with priest skill level 1, fighter skill level 4 (these are adventurer skills), sailor skill level 5, and fisher skill level 2 (these are general skills) would be at adventurer level **4** . 

14

## **1.3.4 Explanation of Adventurer Skills** 

Adventurer skills are the most important thing for an adventurer in _Sword World RPG._ This is because which adventurer skills a character has acquired will determine what that character can do, and will reveal that character's characteristics. 

Below is a brief description of each adventurer skill. 

## 【 **Fighter** 】 

Those with this skill are adept warriors, who fight with weapons. They are experts in combat, able to use all kinds of weapons and armor. 

_A wandering swordsman who makes a name for himself solely on the strength of his sword, and eventually establishes a kingdom._ Such a story is also possible in Forcelia. 

## 【 **Thief** 】 

One who has this skill will be called a _thief_ . As indicated, they bring along abilities that are useful for theft, such as pickpocketing, lock-picking, and so on. 

However, not everyone with the thief skill makes a living off such thefts. The abilities of the thief are also useful in adventures. For example, there's the ability to detect and disable traps that have been set, and the ability to stay ahead of footsteps, listening and observing what’s going on. 

Thieves also have some fighting ability. Their fighting style is based on lightness, and although they cannot use heavy weapons or armor, they _can_ use light weapons and armor that does not impede their movement, allowing them to fight as well as a fighter. 

For this reason, many of them have made a name for themselves as _adventurers_ . 

**Table 1-2: Skill Types**

## 【 **Sage** 】 

## 【 **Bard** 】 

Those with the sage skill are commonly referred to as sages. They're knowledgeable and well-versed in all fields of study (academics in Forcelia are not differentiated, and one is rarely versed in only one specific field). 

Those who play musical instruments and sing songs for a living are called bards. Thus, those with the bard skill have the ability to play an instrument and sing songs. Many legends and folklore are often woven into songs passed down from ancient times, and 

Although they are often thought to be confined to their study rooms, many of them do go out on _adventures_ , because they believe that _the best way to learn is to see and hear firsthand_ . 

**==> picture [146 x 270] intentionally omitted <==**

## 【 **Ranger** 】 

An expert in outdoor activities. Those with this skill are adept at outdoor living, and dealing with a variety of natural hazards. These abilities are, of course, essential for _adventure_ . 

Rangers are also excellent with projectile weapons and have the same offensive capabilities as fighters, especially when it comes to projectiles. 

15

natural bards are familiar with these legends and folklore. 

Bards also have the ability to sing magical songs called **spellsongs** . These songs have magical effects on all who hear them. 

## 【 **Rune Master** 】 

The five adventurer skills of sorcerer, shaman, priest, dark priest, and dragon priest are sometimes collectively referred to as **rune master skills** . This is because these five classes use **runes** and have the ability to handle magic. Rune masters are viewed with fear by the general public because of their magical abilities. 

Bards also use magical abilities through their _spellsongs_ but are not considered rune masters. There is a great difference in the _spells_ that rune masters handle, both in quality and quantity. 

## 【 **Sorcerer** 】 

The ancient kingdom of Kastuul, which fell more than 500 years ago, is sometimes referred to as the _magical kingdom_ . As its nickname suggests, Kastuul is said to have accomplished various miracles through the power of 

**==> picture [147 x 283] intentionally omitted <==**

magic. It is this magical power that is known as **high ancient** and is the magic that the sorcerers of today have inherited. 

With the fall of Kastuul, much of high ancient was lost, but some has survived to the present day. The skill that can handle it is the sorcerer skill. 

Many sorcerers are also sages. The rules do not require one character to acquire both of these skills, but if they are acquired at the same time, it gives them a significant advantage. 

## 【 **Shaman** 】 

It is said that when creating Forcelia, the gods divided the world into three: the _spirit world_ , the _fairy world_ , and the _material world_ . The _spirit world_ is the world of **spirits** who command various powers. The _material world_ is the world in which physical beings like us humans live, and the _fairy world_ is the world that connects the two. 

**Silent spirit** are runes used to communicate with spirits and draw out the powers they control. The skill that can use silent spirit is called the shaman skill, and those who have this skill are referred to as shamans. 

The powers controlled by spirits are diverse, ranging from the four major powers of _earth_ , _water_ , _fire_ , and _wind_ to the _spirit of humans_ , and there are many different types of **spirit magic** used by shamans. 

## 【 **Priest** 】 

Those who exercise their power as agents of the gods are priests. In the ancient days of myth, the gods who created the world were later divided into light and darkness and waged a great war. A great war that engulfed all the ancient races– including the legendary dragons of old --ended with all the gods losing their bodies. 

Gods who have lost their physical bodies cannot engage with the _material world_ , which is the world for those with bodies. Instead, they interact with the world through priests. 

Priests, as agents of the gods, can use **holy prayer** and cast **holy magic** . 

## 【 **Dark Priest** 】 

The gods are divided into two main groups. The **gods of light** and the **gods of darkness** . This is the same classification as the two factions that waged war in ancient times. 

Gods who belonged to the _gods of darkness_ are now considered _wicked gods_ . Each of the gods teaches and guides people, but the teachings and guidance of the _gods of darkness_ are extremely individualistic and deny human reason, because they are far removed from human _morality_ , so to speak. Another hindrance to belief in the wicked gods, is the fact that the majority of people in Forcelia today believe in the teachings of the _gods of light_ . 

However, even the wicked gods have their believers. As agents of the wicked gods, there are dark priests who use **daemon scream** and cast **dark magic** , which is essentially equivalent to Divine Magic. Players may _not_ choose the dark priest skill as one of their character's skills. This is because it would be significantly more difficult to have a character practice the teachings of the _gods of darkness_ , when examining a roleplaying game from the _role-playing_ perspective. 

## 【 **Dragon Priest** 】 

The runes used by dragon priests are called **dragon roar** . 

Dragon roar is a system of magic that imbues one's own body with the characteristics of a dragon, and the ultimate goal is to transform oneself into a dragon. Therefore, they worship dragons and try to live like them. 

Players may _not_ choose the dragon priest skill as one of their character's skills. This is because it would be extremely difficult to have a character live the life of a dragon priest, when examining a role-playing game from the _role-playing_ perspective.

# 1.4 Character Creation ## **1.4** ## **Character Creation** Now it's time to show you how to create a _player character_ who will explore Forcelia as the player's alter ego. Please prepare Character Sheets. You may photocopy the one at the end of this book (photocopying is permitted for personal use only). A Character Sheet is used to write down various information about a character during the course of the game. It is designed to assist in character creation as well. ## **1.4.1** ## **Choose a Race** First, the player character's race must be chosen. There are five types to choose from: human, dwarf, grassrunner, elf, and half-elf. Each race has their own characteristics and limitations. Each also has strengths and weaknesses in the ability scores that form the basis of the character. ## 【 **Human** 】 Humans have the most average ability scores and can do almost anything without a hitch. They have no restrictions on skill selection. This is probably the most suitable race for a beginner to choose. ## 【 **Dwarf** 】 Dwarves excel in strength, life force, and mental power. On the other hand, they tend to be less agile and less intelligent. In terms of skills, sorcerer and shaman cannot be chosen. ## 【 **Grassrunner** 】 Grassrunners have excellent dexterity, agility, and mental power. Their strength, on the other hand, is decidedly inferior. Their intelligence and life force are average. Rune master skills cannot be acquired. At the same time, their low strength does not let them benefit much from acquiring the fighter skill. It's a good idea to advance the thief skill acquired from the beginning. If they were raised by elves, there is a restriction that they cannot acquire the priest skill. On the other hand, if they were raised by humans, there is no limit on acquiring skills. ## 【 **Elf** 】 ## **Example:** Elves excel in dexterity, agility, intelligence, and mental power. On the other hand, their strength and life force tend to be low. There is a restriction that they cannot acquire the priest skill. It would be better to advance the shaman skill acquired from the beginning, or to acquire the sorcerer skill. They are also a suitable race for thief. They're not very suitable for fighter. Now, let's create a character through a real example. First, a race must be chosen. In other words, it is assumed that there are no differences or changes in ability based on gender or age. Of course, no matter what name you choose, it will not affect their abilities. Since the character we're creating is a human, we'll use the _backgrounds_ table. Our roll is "9". The character ended up coming from the _mercenary_ background. Therefore, we'll have 1 level in the fighter skill as our starting skill, and 2500 starting experience points. In addition, money is listed as 2Dx200. This means we roll 2D and have money (in **gamels** ) equal to the result times 200. The result of our next 2D roll is _6_ . The character has 1200 gamels. ## 《 **Age** 》 However, you’re not completely free to decide on age. It is basically impossible to play as a child who has not fully grown both physically and mentally, or an old man who is clearly on the physical decline. In addition, in _Sword World RPG_ , newly created characters are _green youths_ , so to speak, who don’t have much experience. Therefore, making them _too old_ poses a problem in this sense as well. ## **1.4.4** ## **Age, Gender, Name** A character must be given a name, and their age and gender must be decided. These things may be decided at any stage of character creation. You can decide at the beginning. When deciding on the age of a character, it should be between 15 years old and around 30 years old at most for a human. The life span of a human is about 100 years old. Of course, it's natural that they'll die earlier than this if they're ill or wounded. In Alecrast, the average life span of a human is about 50 years due to underdeveloped medical care. ## 《 **Ages of Each Race** 》 For races other than humans, consider age as follows. Even for different races with long life spans, the rate of growth to adulthood is not much different from that of humans. Therefore, once they reach around 20 years of age, they are physically fit enough to embark on an adventure. However, it is one thing to be physically mature and another to be socially recognized as an adult. In Japan, where we live, we are considered adults at the age of 20, but there are some actions that are recognized as adult at the age of 18. There was a time when the socially accepted age of adulthood was about 15 years old, and even younger. Whether or not a person is recognized as an adult is not only a matter of physical growth, but also a matter of social judgment. In the human societies of _Alecrast_ , 15 years old is considered to be the age of adulthood. However, in the societies of other races, you basically have to be older to be considered an adult, and the longer the life span of the race, the later they tend to do so. As a result, the time for characters of other races to be recognized as adults and have the opportunity to go on adventures will be significantly later than it is for humans; around 30 years of age for dwarves, 40 years for grassrunners, and 100 for elves. Half-elves, if raised in human society, are treated as adults at around age 15, just like humans. This is because it’s considered common sense in human society. In elf society, it’s a little later. However, because they are clearly known 19 to have a shorter lifespan than elves and because they are essentially detested, they are not made to wait until they are 100 years old and are forced out of society at around age 30. Use this as a guide when deciding on the age of a character of another race. ## 《 **Gender** 》 As mentioned before, _Sword World RPG_ treats men and women as having no difference in ability. Therefore, the choice of gender is completely free. Even races other than humans have two genders, male and female, exactly the same as humans. Also consider the differences in appearance between men and women, as well as the differences in the division of roles in society, to be equivalent to those of humans. ## 《 **Name** 》 Naturally, a character must have a name. It's not convenient to enjoy the fictional world of Forcelia if they're always called by the player's name or just _hey.... you_ . Therefore, a character should be given a name that is appropriate for Forcelia. When deciding on a name for a character, it is usually best to stick with a basic western-style name. After all, the names of most people in _Alecrast_ , the setting of _Sword World RPG_ , are like that. Purely Japanese or Chinese names are not suitable. In _Alecrast_ , first name, then last name are usually written in that order. In rare cases, a middle name may also be added between the first and last names. Alternatively, it's acceptable to simply decide on a first name and leave the last unspecified, or to use a nickname or alias, as is often used by thieves. ## **Example:** Let’s decide on the age, gender, and name of the character we're creating. Since they're a human, their age will be around 15~30 years old. This This should be understood as _purchasing skills with experience points_ . character comes from the _mercenary_ background, so being too young is not an option. Therefore, the age should be a little higher, like around 24. It is also possible to increase the level of skills that have already been acquired. You may even acquire 2 or more levels at a time, so long as there are enough experience points to spend. Note, however, that experience points are spent at each level attained. For example, if you do not have the ranger skill and you want to acquire 2 levels in ranger at once, you must spend 500 + 1000 for a total of 1500 experience points. Either gender is acceptable. Here, we'll put _man_ . Although there are many women who fight in Forcelia, mercenaries will probably still have a higher percentage of men. As for the name, it’s up to the player to decide on a name. You may follow your own sensibilities, or you may borrow the name of a real westerner. In this section, we'll use _Ducard Sample_ `Ⅱ` (the second), though it's not a very sensible name. If you have enough experience points, you can acquire multiple skills. Only adventurer skills can be acquired or increased using experience points. General skills cannot be acquired or increased with experience points. ## **1.4.5** ## **Character Growth via** In addition, starting experience points do not have to be used up. If a character has neither the sorcerer nor sage skill, they can acquire 1 level in both the sorcerer and sage skills by spending 2000 experience points. You may acquire sorcerer only, but it's better to avoid this, as it will put you at a disadvantage during the game. If a character has 1 level in the sage skill (so if they're a human or half-elf, and if _merchant/scholar_ or _noble_ was rolled on Table 1-5: Backgrounds), they can acquire 1 level in the sorcerer skill for 1500 points. Of course, having the sage skill does not mean that you're required to have the sorcerer skill. The sage skill is useful enough on its own. It is important to note that _it is against the rules for a character who has neither the sorcerer nor sage skills to acquire only the sorcerer skill for 1500 points._ Table 1-7: Experience Points by Skill (at Character Creation) is almost identical to a segment of Table 8-1: Experience Points by Skill, which is used for character growth as explained in chapter 8. Even swords of the same shape are sold in a variety of sizes to match the physical strength of the person using it. In real life, when a baseball player chooses a bat for himself, he chooses one that matches his physical strength. For an adventurer, the first step to survival is to choose a weapon most suited to their strength. Basically, the most suitable weapon is one whose _required strength equals the character's strength_ . As long as you choose that, you are guaranteed the same attack capability, even if you use a different type of weapon. Think of a sword, ax, or spear with a required strength of 14 as the same in terms of game value. Therefore, it’s a good idea to choose one based on the image of your character. However, most weapons have a limited range of required strength. It’s a constraint that comes with the general size and nature of the weapon. Choose non-metal armor or ring mail. 23 ## **[ Sorcerer ]** If you want to make use of your sorcerer skill, your armor must be _cloth_ or _soft leather_ . Either of these should be the one with the greatest required strength within the character's strength range. Also, you should not buy a shield. For weapons, be sure to purchase a **mage’s staff** . This is essential when using **ancient magic** . If the character's strength is 10 or less, it is appropriate to buy one with the same required strength as the character's strength; if the character's strength is 10 or greater, it is appropriate to buy one with a required strength of 10. ## **[ Shaman ]** If you want to make use of your shaman skill, your armor is limited to _cloth_ , _soft leather_ , or _hard leather_ . Of these, the one with the greatest required strength within the character's strength range should be chosen. Also, you should not buy a shield. When choosing a weapon, since you’re not buying a shield, it's a good idea to choose a _two-handed_ weapon if at all possible. It is fundamental to match your strength to the required strength of the weapon. ## **[ Sage, Bard, Priest ]** These skills have no weapon or armor restrictions. Refer to restrictions on other skills acquired at the same time. If a character has acquired only these skills, the basic idea is to choose weapons and armor whose required strength is the same as their own strength. ## **Example:** Ducard II has 2 levels in the fighter skill and 1 level in the ranger skill. There are no restrictions on either skill when choosing a weapon. Ranger has an advantage in projectiles, but this is consequently irrelevant because of the higher level of the _can use any weapon_ fighter. So, when choosing a weapon, we'll choose one that has a required strength equal to the character's strength (14). Here we have chosen the most typical weapon, a **broadsword** . The required strength of a broadsword is 8~16, so a broadsword with a required strength of 14 certainly exists. The price is 14 x 20 + 40 = **320** (gamels). When choosing armor, the ranger skill's limitations come into play (required strength can only be up to _half_ one's own strength, armor can only be _non-metal_ or _ring mail_ ). Our character's strength is 14, so we can buy armor with a required strength of 7. Here we will choose **ring mail** . The price is 7 x 30 + 30 = **240** (gamels). Since a broadsword is a _one-handed_ weapon, this character can _also_ hold a shield. If we have a large shield, the ranger skill will be limited, so our choice here is limited to a **small shield** . The price is **60** gamels. Since our starting amount was 1200 gamels, our character now has 1200 - 320 - 240 - 60 = **580 gamels** remaining. ## **1.4.8** ## **Purchasing Other Equipment** If you only have weapons and armor, you will not be able to go on an adventure. It won’t be an adventure if you forget your ropes and torches. For equipment other than weapons and armor, see Table 1-11: Equipment. ## **[ Weight Limits ]** Many games set some kind of _weight_ on weapons, armor and equipment, limiting the amount of goods a character can carry. However, _Sword World RPG_ does not do this. Instead, please use your _common sense_ to make decisions. A character cannot have an infinite number of things. It is impossible to hold 1000 torches or 1 kilometer of rope. This would be a matter of course even if the rules do not stipulate it. ## **Example:** Ducard II has 580 gamels left. How much equipment can we buy with this? First, we'll need a rucksack (50G). Without it, it'll be difficult to carry luggage. We should also have a belt pouch (15G) for small items and money. Since we’re going on an adventure, a water bag (20G) is a must. If we're planning to sleep outdoors, we'll need a mantle (40G) and a blanket (50G). A tent is too expensive and we don't need something large enough for five people. If he has friends, though, it would be nice if we could pool our money together to buy it. Tableware (12G) and cooking utensils (50G) will also be needed for camping. For a lighting tool, we’ll first need a tinderbox (20G). Without that, a fire cannot be started. We’ll also need torches (6 for 5 G). A lantern would be brighter, but money is tight and we can't afford that luxury. Riding horses or pack horses aren’t possible due to their price. Now let's buy some parchment (10 sheets for 10 G) and quill pen ink (5 G). Ducard II's ranger skill includes the _map creation_ ability. It may be useful at some point. The thieves' tools are for a thief and the musical instruments are for a bard, so they're not necessary for Ducard II. So far, 277 gamels have been spent on purchases, with 303 gamels remaining. We won’t buy preserved food yet. This will help keep the game running smoothly. In fact, a Character Sheet saves time and eliminates mistakes in character creation. Let us explain how to fill out the sheet according to the order of character creation. ## **[** ① **Player Name ]** The player should write their own name in the player name field. ## **[** ② **Race ]** The first decision in character creation is race. Once you've decided, please write it here. ## **[** ③ **Sub-Ability Scores]** Once race is chosen, sub-ability scores can be determined. Roll the dice according to Table 1-3: Determining Sub-Ability Scores and write down the sub-ability scores from A-H in order. ## **[** ④ **Ability Scores ]** Once sub-ability scores are determined, ability scores are calculated by addition. Please calculate and write down the six ability scores. ## **[** ⑤ **Ability Bonuses ]** Once ability scores have been calculated, the next step is the ability bonuses. A bonus is an ability score divided by 6 (rounded down). ## **[** ⑥ **Origin ]** If the character is a human or a halfelf raised by a human, Table 1-5: Backgrounds must be used to determine their origin. Once it's been decided, write it in the _origin_ field. If the character was not raised in a human society, there is no need to write anything in this field. ## **[** ⑦ **Starting Experience Points ]** Please write down starting experience points as determined by Table 1-5: Backgrounds or Table 1-6: Starting Experience by Race in the _experience points_ field. ## **[** ⑧ **Starting Skills ]** As with starting experience points, write down your starting skills. It’s advisable to write them in the order of adventurer skill → general skill. ## **[** ⑨ **Money ]** Table 1-5: Backgrounds or Table 1-6: Starting Experience by Race determine the amount of money (2D x _modifier_ ) that you have. Roll the dice, calculate it, and write it down in the _money_ field. ## **[** ⑩ **Gender ]** Once the process has been completed up to this point, you should have developed an image of the character. It should now be easier to decide on gender and age, plus the character's name. Decide on gender and write it in this field. ## **[** ⑪ **Age ]** As with gender, decide on an age and write it down. ## **[** ⑫ **Character Name ]** Give your character a name, and write it down here. ## **[** ⑬ **Increase Skills ]** Starting experience points can be used to acquire new adventurer skills or to increase any skills already acquired (Table 1-7: Experience Points by Skill). If this changes experience points and adventurer skill levels, rewrite them in the appropriate fields, respectively. ## **[** ⑭ **Adventurer Level ]** Once you’ve acquired your final skills (starting skill and growth via starting experience points), you can determine your adventurer level. Adventurer level is equal to the highest adventurer skill level. Write this number down. ## **[** ⑮ **Purchase Equipment]** Use your money to purchase equipment. Write down the items you purchased then rewrite your money, reducing by the amount of money you spent (Table 1-11: Equipment). When you purchase weapons, shields, and armor, write them in the appropriate fields (Table 1-8: Weapons, Table 1-9: Armor, and Table 1-10: Shields). ## **[** ⑯ **Other Sections ]** The character sheet is not completely filled out yet. However, to fill in anything other than what’s explained here, you must understand the rules a bit more in detail. So for now, this is the end of the instructions for filling out the sheet. # 2.3 Checks

## **Baseline Scores** 

## **2.1** 

_skill level_ , and their dexterity bonus is used for _ability bonus_ . 

A **baseline score** is a number that indicates how competent or skilled a character is at a given activity. 

For example, if you want to hit an enemy with your sword, it is important to have dexterity to wield it accurately. Also, if the character is trained or experienced as a warrior, they’ll be even more likely to hit their target. 

## **Example:** 

Let’s find the baseline score for Ducard II when he wields his sword. His fighter skill level is 2 and his dexterity bonus is +2. 

Therefore, 2+2= **4** is his baseline score. 

In _Sword World RPG_ , this is expressed as **relevant skill level + ability bonus** and is called the _baseline score_ . 

Fighter is only useful in combat. The sorcerer, shaman, and priest rune master skills are also of little use for anything other than casting magic. 

**baseline score** = relevant skill level + ability bonus 

Other skills are used in a variety of situations. See Chapter 6: Adventurer 

For example, if the player wields a sword, their fighter skill level is used for 

## **Target Scores** 

## **2.2** 

The baseline score is a number that represents a character's ability. However, the success of an action is not only related to the ability of the person attempting it, but also to the difficulty of the action itself. The difficulty of hitting a target with the same weapon is much greater when your opponent is a seasoned warrior, than when he is an amateur with no fighting experience. 

This is represented by a **target score** . The greater the target score, the more difficult the action. 

Target scores for certain actions can be found in _Part 2: Game Master Section_ . This is because it is the game master who sets the target scores, not the players. 

## **Checks** 

## **2.3** 

Once the baseline score and target score are determined, a success roll **check** can finally be made. 

## **Example:** 

Did Ducard II’s attack hit the Goblin? His baseline score for attacks is _4_ and the target score for hitting a goblin is _10_ . In other words, we must roll two dice, add 4 to the result, and if the total is _10 or more_ , we’ll hit the target. 

The player must roll two dice and add their baseline score to the roll. The result is called the **final score** . This final score should be _equal to or greater than_ the target score. 

Try rolling the dice yourself. If the roll is 6 or greater, it’s a _hit_ , and if the roll is 5 or lower, it’s a _miss_ . 

final score (baseline score + 2D) ≥ target score → **action is successful** 

final score (baseline score + 2D) < target score → **action fails** 

Skills Other Than Combat and Magic to see which skills can be used for the baseline score of any given action. It also lists the ability scores associated with those actions. 

If a character does not have any skills related to an action, the baseline score is **0** regardless of their ability scores. 

## **Example:** 

Let’s say Ducard II is attempting to unlock a door. The two skills he has-fighter and ranger skill --do not have the ability _lockpicking_ . This means that Ducard II does not have a skill associated with picking locks. In this case, his dexterity bonus (+2) has no effect and his baseline score is _0_ . 

## **Example:** 

Ducard II is fighting some goblins. He swings his sword and cuts at the monster in front of him first. 

The game master declares the target score of this action to be _10_ . 

## **2.3.1** 

## **Automatic Success and Automatic Failure** 

Even the most difficult acts can succeed by a quirk of fate. On the other hand, no matter how simple something may seem, it may fail due to some misfortune. This is represented by the **automatic success** and **automatic failure** rules. 

30

On a success roll, if a player rolls a **12** , i.e., both dice roll 6 ( **double sixes** ), the action is a _success_ , no matter how low the baseline score nor how high the target score. 

Basically, automatic success and automatic failure are _not_ definitive success or definitive failure. It simply means that you succeeded or failed, _not_ that you succeeded very _well_ or failed very _badly_ . 

Conversely, if that player rolls a **2** , that is, if the two dice both roll a 1 ( **double ones** ), the action _fails_ , no matter 

## **Bonuses and Penalties** 

## **2.4** 

A success roll may be subject to modifiers. This is to replicate any favorable or unfavorable circumstances when a character is about to perform an action. 

In such cases, the game master applies a **bonus** or **penalty** to the success roll. Things like, _+2 because they're falling over_ , or _-4 because it's dark_ . 

When such bonuses or penalties are applied, the baseline score will go up or down. A player must also calculate these before making a success roll. 

For example, if the person you’re fighting is falling, their movements will be restricted and your attacks will be easier to land. On the other hand, if you’re fighting in the dark, then unless your character has darkvision, you cannot see your opponent, so it is not so easy to hit him. 

final score {baseline score + 2D ± (bonus, penalty)} ≥ target score → **action is successful** 

## **Difficulty Checks** 

## **2.5** 

So, confident that there are no traps, you open the door and move forward....... 

Consider the following case here: 

You (your character) are now at a door. The owner of the mansion is known to be paranoid and is rumored to have set numerous traps. So you decide to check for traps, thinking that carelessly opening this door might activate a trap....... 

_Wait a minute_ . RPG stands for _role playing_ game. In other words, the game is played as if _you_ were the role or character assigned to you. What if we look at the trap-finding scene from the _character's_ point of view? 

The _find trap_ ability of the thief skill will be used to see if this will work. That means your success roll will use _thief skill level + intelligence bonus_ as the baseline score. 

You examined the door. You followed the door with your eyes for any odd nicks, tapped lightly on the door to hear the echo, and carefully twisted the knob slightly to see if it felt right. As a result, you did not discover any traps. 

Let's assume that this baseline score is _4_ . The game master then declares the target score to be _12_ . 

_There are no traps_ . 

You tell your companions so. But one of your companions asks you with a difficult look on his face: 

You (the player) roll the dice. The roll is _8_ . 4+8= _12_ , so it's a success, although just barely. 

_Really? The owner of this place is the kind of guy who'd think you're a thief when he sees you_ . _You just couldn't find a trap, could you? Maybe the traps are so_ 

The game master says _You couldn't find anything_ . 

Some games treat extreme stakes during an action check as a _definitive_ success or failure, and _Sword World RPG_ may do so as well. However, if such a process is _not_ specified, it is _not_ treated as a definitive success/failure. Do be careful. 

final score {baseline score + 2D ± (bonus, penalty)} < target score → **action failed** 

How bonuses and penalties are applied can be found in _Part 2: Game Master Section_ . This is because it’s the game master who determines these things, not the players. 

Even if a bonus or penalty is applied, the roll is still an automatic success if the roll comes up _double sixes_ , and an automatic failure if the roll if it’s _double ones_ . 

_well hidden that you just couldn't see them?_ 

Can you answer this question? Can you answer the question from your character's point of view? _I landed the success roll_ is not an answer. That's an answer from the _player's_ perspective, not the character's. 

That's right. Within the setting, there are actions that we attempt on our own but _do not know_ (or _cannot tell_ ) whether they are successful or unsuccessful. 

These checks-- which are often made mainly when finding something -- change the method of success rolls considerably. The **difficulty** is determined via target score determination. 

31

## **2.5.1 Difficulty** 

**Difficulty** is a score determined by the game master based on the difficulty of the actions the character attempts. What makes this different from a target score is that this is _not_ the direct number the player is aiming for. 

## **2.5.2** 

## **How to Make a Difficulty Check** 

To make a **difficulty check** , the game master also rolls the dice (2D). This roll is then added to the _difficulty_ and used as the target score. The game master does not reveal their roll nor the target score that results from their calculation, to the players. Whether the base difficulty is revealed or not depends on the situation. 

The players' dice rolls are otherwise the same. They will be told the baseline score, roll 2D, and modify it with any bonuses or penalties to find a final score. 

The game master then takes the final score and compares it to the target score (difficulty + 2D) that they have secretly set. If there is a tie or if the final score is greater, the action is a _success_ ; if it is less, the action is a _failure_ . 

final score ≥ target score (difficulty + 2D) → **action succeeds** 

final score < target score (difficulty + 2D) → **action fails** 

The game master does not say whether the result is _the action succeeded_ or _the action failed_ , because a difficulty check is made when the success or failure of the action is _not known_ to the characters. They must only answer as much as the character can tell from their successes and failures. Let's show how the game master's answer would be for the case of the door and the traps: 

1. There _is_ a trap, the find trap check is _successful_ → _There is a trap at the door_ 2. There _is_ a trap, the find trap check is a _failure_ → _You don't find any traps_ 

3. There is _no_ trap, the find trap check is _successful_ → _You don't find any traps_ 4. There is _no_ trap, the find trap check is a _failure_ → _You don't find any traps_ 

In the case of #2, if you assume there are no traps, you will have a terrible time. 

## **2.5.3** 

## **Analogy of Success and Failure** 

If a success roll is made for a difficulty check, the success or failure of the action is strictly unknown to the player. That is precisely the reason why we use difficulty checks. 

However, a player is given only _one_ criteria to determine whether the success roll they made for a difficulty check is a success or a failure. 

That is: the dice that the players themselves have rolled. 

If the roll is as high as 10 or 11, the success roll is considered to have a very high chance of success. Therefore, if after examining the door, you do not find a trap, the risk is quite low even if you assume that there really are no traps. 

If the roll is low, such as 3 or 4, then there's a high chance that the success roll has failed. So, even if you examine the door and do not find any traps, you should be skeptical of the results. 

In particular, when a player rolls either of the two extremes, _double sixes_ or _double ones_ , it means _automatic success_ or _automatic failure_ , so that the player can clearly see whether the success roll is successful or not. 

A player's dice roll should be interpreted as an expression of _confidence_ in the character's actions. A high roll indicates a high level of 

confidence, while a low roll indicates a lack of confidence in what they've done. 

## **Caution** 

It has been noted before that _double sixes_ and _double ones_ are _automatic_ successes or failures and do not imply _definitive_ successes or failures. 

It is especially bad to misinterpret an automatic failure as a _definitive_ failure. For example, if a player is trying to find a trap on a door and has rolled double ones, the game master will of course tell the player _you didn't find any traps_ . When the player hears this, if they reply like the following, it's a prime example of this misinterpretation: _Oh, I screwed up big time. Which means I can say for sure “There are definitely no traps on this door”. And yet I'll carelessly open it and get hit._ *laughs* 

This is misinterpreting the rules. Instead, 

_I couldn’t find any traps, but I’m not too confident in my search. Consider it like I never checked the door._ 

would be the correct thing to say.

# 2.7 Resistance Rolls

## **2.6** 

## **Success Rolls Using Adventurer Level** 

For a success roll, the baseline score is usually calculated as _relevant skill level + ability bonus_ . However, in some cases, _adventurer level_ , rather than skill level, is used to calculate the baseline score. 

For actions that _anyone_ would be able to perform if they became proficient in 

adventuring, the baseline score for a success roll is _adventurer level + ability bonus_ . 

Actions that fit into this category are explained in _6.6: Common Adventurer Abilities_ . In addition, the following _2.7: Resistance Rolls_ are also a check that uses 

adventurer level in its baseline score calculation. 

When a character with adventurer level 0 (a character with no adventurer skills) makes these checks, the baseline score is _0_ regardless of their ability scores. 

## **2.7** 

## **Resistance Rolls** 

A success roll made using _adventurer level + life force bonus_ as the baseline score is specifically referred to as a **life force resistance roll** . Similarly, a success roll made using _adventurer level + mental power bonus_ as the baseline score is specifically called a **mental power resistance roll** , and the two are collectively called simply **resistance rolls** . 

The reason for the name change and the distinction between the two in particular, is that they are frequently performed. 

Therefore, these baseline scores are also defined by the terms **life force resistance** and **mental power resistance** , among others. 

Life force resistance rolls are made to avoid or reduce the effects of physical dangers such as poison, while mental power resistance rolls are made to avoid or reduce the effects of magic or curses. 

In particular, all checks against magic, even if it causes physical damage, are made with mental power resistance rolls. Checks against special attacks by monsters that are magical in nature are also made with mental power resistance rolls.

Since Ducard II has an adventurer level of 2 and a +2 mental power bonus, the baseline score of his mental power resistance roll (mental power resistance power) is _4_ . While touring an underground cave on an adventure, he wanders into a mysterious room where all four walls are painted in extreme colors. 

In fact, there is a magic trap set in this room that will put anyone who enters it into a deep sleep. The richly colored patterns in the room begin to move strangely, and Ducard II is struck by a strong sense of drowsiness. The game master asks us to make a mental power resistance roll against target score 17. Normally, this would never succeed, but our 2D roll is _12_ ! 

It’s an automatic success. He withstands the magic and escapes the room safely. **Filling Out the Character Sheet**

To the right of each of these is a field to write down the bonus, and further to the right of that should be a field labeled _resistance_ . Write the baseline scores of each resistance roll here . 

The baseline score (resistance) equals _adventurer level + life force/mental power bonus_ .

## **Action Announcements** 

## **3.1**

## **3.1.1** 

## **Order of Action Announcements** 

If there are three or more groups instead of two, the order of action announcements is determined in the same way. The groups with the higher intelligence can decide on their actions later than the others. 

Each round begins with an announcement of what your character intends to do. 

In combat, there are basically two opposing groups. **Action announcements** are made by each group. 

In an action announcement, you cannot declare something impossible. Casting a spell while running at full speed is prohibited by the rules. If there's an enemy in front of you that’s attacking you, attacking another enemy several meters away is not possible, just by common sense. These impossible actions cannot be declared, as a matter of course. 

Suppose there are three characters, a1, a2, and a3 in **group A** and two characters, b1 and b2 in **group B** . In this case, the order of action announcements is **A → B** or **B → A** . Characters who belong to different groups, such as **a1→b1→a2** ...... do _not_ take turns to announce their actions. 

Which of the two will make their action announcements first is determined based on _intelligence_ . The group with the _highest_ intelligence among all the characters (including monsters, etc.) participating in combat will announce their action _later_ . If _a2_ is the one with the highest intelligence between groups A and B as mentioned, then the announcements are made in the order of _B → A_ , regardless of the intelligence of the other characters. Those who announce their actions later- the ones with highest representative intelligence --will have a significant advantage because they can announce their own actions _after_ hearing their opponents' announcements. 

## **3.1.2** 

## **Player Action Announcements** 

A player must be specific when announcing their action. 

Instead of _attack with my weapon_ , they should say _attack the rightmost goblin with my sword_ . 

Instead of _use magic_ , they should say 

_cast a_ _**fireball** spell._ 

You must be specific in this way. If the game master asks a question, be sure to answer it clearly. 

## **3.1.3** 

## **NPC and Monster Action Announcements** 

If the two groups are _equal_ in intelligence (i.e., the highest intelligence in each group is the same), compare the _second_ highest in each group. If those are _also_ the same, then compare the _third_ highest. 

When NPCs, monsters, etc. announce their actions, it’s done by the game master who manages them. Specific action announcements may not be made at this time. 

If a settlement cannot be reached, the game master gets to announce their group's actions first (NPCs and monsters) and then the players follow. 

For example, simply say, _I am about to open my big mouth_ . They won't know if it’s opening its mouth to bite, or perhaps even breathe fire. 

The order in which action announcements are made within the 

You may only declare, _I'm preparing to cast a spell_ , and not give out the specific name of the spell. This is the difference in position between the players and the game master. The game master, as the person who manages the game, must know the actions of all the characters and monsters that appear. However, the players should only act from their _characters’_ point of view. So, if a _character_ cannot predict something, the _player_ cannot know it either. When you see it for the first time, you won't know how a monster you don't recognize will act, and when an enemy tries to cast a spell you don't recognize, you won't know what that spell does. 

Of course, if a character has a wealth of knowledge, or if they’re an advanced spellcaster, they _might_ know such things. If it's something the character _should_ know, the game master should tell you when they announce the action.

## **Delaying an Action** 

Characters (including NPCs and monsters) do not have to act based on their agility and can instead delay their actions. 

For example, suppose you know that a fellow wizard can use magic to increase the attack power of your sword. But what if your agility is higher than theirs? The support magic will be applied after you swing your sword, and the magic will be wasted. 

In such a case, a character may lower their **action order** to **0** . In other words, they will act only at the very _end_ of the round. 

If you want to **delay** an action, you must mention it in the action announcement phase, along with the action you intend to take. 

In this case, an intended attack on an enemy that is about to withdraw can be changed to another target that you’re engaged with. 

If the enemy that's trying to withdraw has a higher agility than you, you _must_ switch to a different target, because according to your action order, the enemy will have escaped. At this time, an attacker can choose any new target to switch to (from among those they're engaged with). 

If _your_ agility is higher than that of the enemy who is trying to withdraw, you can attack them before they escape. However, even at this time, it’s still acceptable to switch attack targets. As we'll see later, if all the enemies you’re
